sections.heading: Complications
sections.content:
Recurrent pleural effusion causing dyspnea and chest discomfort.
Chest wall pain from recurrent nodules or pleural disease.
Bronchial or esophageal obstruction.
Hematologic toxicities (neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia) are common with platinum/pemetrexed regimens [1,2,3,4].
Fatigue, nausea/vomiting, and mucositis are typical side effects managed per NCCN guidelines [PM-B].
Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) such as pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies, and skin reactions can occur with nivolumab, ipilimumab, or pembrolizumab. Management follows the NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities [MS-7, PM-B].
Infusion-related reactions are possible.
Bevacizumab is associated with increased rates of grade 3-4 hypertension (23% vs. 0%), proteinuria (3% vs. 0%), and thrombotic events (6% vs. 1%) when added to chemotherapy. It is contraindicated in patients with uncontrolled hypertension, bleeding/clotting risk, or significant cardiovascular morbidity [MS-5].
Pleurectomy/decortication (P/D) and extrapleural pneumonectomy (EPP) carry significant perioperative morbidity and mortality. The MARS trial noted a perioperative mortality of 12.5% for EPP [187].
Lung-sparing P/D is associated with lower perioperative mortality than EPP but still carries risks [MS-9, MS-10].
Radiation pneumonitis is a significant risk, especially with pleural IMRT. Fatal pneumonitis can occur if strict dose limits to the contralateral lung are not applied [13, MS-11].
The contralateral mean lung dose should be kept <8.5 Gy, and contralateral V20 <7% [15, MS-11, PM-D].
Other toxicities include esophagitis, fatigue, and skin reactions.
